coredata.dc:title: A novel AgCl-based visible-light photocatalyst through in-situ assembly of carbon dots for efficient dye degradation and hydrogen evolution
coredata.dc:creator: Tang, Yuanyuan
Wang, Wentai
Wang, Bozheng
Sun, Xing
Guo, Chunyu
Xu, Jia
coredata.dc:description: A novel efficient AgCl-based heterojunction photocatalyst was synthesized by in-situ electrostatic-assembly of carbon dots (CDs) for the first time. Distinct from the physical blending between CDs and AgCl (CDs + AgCl), CDsAg was heterojuncted into AgCl in this work. The resulting CDsAg-AgCl photocatalyst was endowed with significantly enhanced visible-light photocatalytic performances in both dye degradation and hydrogen evolution With a Cl−/Ag+ molar percentage of 90% and visible light irradiation, methyl orange (MO) was degraded within 30 min with a degradation efficiency of 0.069 min−1, which was about 42.3, 8.9 and 10.6 times higher than those of CDs, AgCl and CDsAg+AgCl, respectively. It also yielded an excellent hydrogen evolution efficiency of 617.4 μmolg−1 h−1, exhibiting a high potential in the field of energy regeneration. Moreover, it showed a good reusability and stability after 5 recycles for both degradation and hydrogen evolution. In addition, a possible photocatalytic mechanism was proposed, where CDsAg-AgCl heterojunction structure facilitated the transfer-separation of photo-generated electron-hole pairs to promote the photocatalytic performance. This work could provide a new route to prepare novel high-efficient photocatalysts for the energy and environmental application.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel efficient AgCl-based heterojunction photocatalyst was synthesized by in-situ electrostatic-assembly of carbon dots (CDs) for the first time. Distinct from the physical blending between CDs and AgCl (CDs + AgCl), CDsAg was heterojuncted into AgCl in this work. The resulting CDsAg-AgCl photocatalyst was endowed with significantly enhanced visible-light photocatalytic performances in both dye degradation and hydrogen evolution With a Cl−/Ag+ molar percentage of 90% and visible light irradiation, methyl orange (MO) was degraded within 30 min with a degradation efficiency of 0.069 min−1, which was about 42.3, 8.9 and 10.6 times higher than those of CDs, AgCl and CDsAg+AgCl, respectively. It also yielded an excellent hydrogen evolution efficiency of 617.4 μmolg−1 h−1, exhibiting a high potential in the field of energy regeneration. Moreover, it showed a good reusability and stability after 5 recycles for both degradation and hydrogen evolution. In addition, a possible photocatalytic mechanism was proposed, where CDsAg-AgCl heterojunction structure facilitated the transfer-separation of photo-generated electron-hole pairs to promote the photocatalytic performance. This work could provide a new route to prepare novel high-efficient photocatalysts for the energy and environmental application.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, serious water and air pollution have been caused by recklessly discarding of wastewater and the overused of fossil fuel with the development of industry and civilization [1–3]. The effluents of these pollutants cannot be sufficiently treated through eco-friendly and economical processes such as adsorption, ozonation and chlorination, so it is necessary to develop novel, cheap and efficient techniques [4–7]. Photocatalysis is regarded as one of the most effective approach for efficient solar energy conversion and utilization in environmental remediation, water splitting for hydrogen generation and CO2 conversion, which has attracted intensive focus from the research communities [8–10]. Although the traditional TiO2 photocatalyst has been extensively investigated and applied, the various disadvantages such as the wide band gap (3.20 eV), rapid recombination of photoinduced electron-hole carriers and none visible light response (48% of the solar spectrum) severely restrict its practical application as high-efficient photocatalyst [11–14].

Recently, silver halide (AgX, X = Cl, Br, I), especially AgCl based photocatalyst, has become a research focus in visible light catalysis field [15]. Constructing heterostructures with other components with matched conduction and valence band can extend visible light absorption range and improve photo-induced electron-hole pairs separation and interfacial transfer efficiency, therefore, leading to higher photocatalytic performance [16,17].Zhang et al. [18] reported a novel efficient Ag@AgCl/g-C3N4 visible light photocatalyst, which exhibited 16.8 times higher rhodamine B (RhB) degradation rate constant than Ag/AgCl. Luo et al. [19] fabricated Ag/AgCl/rGO photocatalyst with heterostructure exhibited much higher photocatalytic activity than physically mixed Ag/AgCl/rGO composite for degrading methyl orange (MO). The enhanced photocatalytic performance of Ag/AgCl/rGO heterostructures can be partially attributed to the effective separation of photogenerated carriers via strongly coupled rGO cocatalyst. Most of the studies on Ag/AgCl composites are focused on the photodegradation of pollutants. Yet it is rare reported that AgCl-based composites are applied in H2 evolution from water splitting under visible-light irradiation.

Carbon dots (CDs) is another rising star of carbon nanomaterials, which has attracted considerable attention due to the unique optical and electronic properties in the fields including photocatalysis [20,21]. CDs is a good electron donor and acceptor with photo-induced electron transfer properties, hence can reduce the recombination of charge carriers [22,23]. Some kinds of CDs exhibit upconversion photoluminescence property, which may be useful in photocatalysis as it may facilitate the utilization of solar energy by other semiconductors, generating more charge carriers, and thus enhance the photocatalytic efficiency [24,25]. Miao et al. [26] prepared nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) functionalized g-C3N4/Ag3PO4/NCDs photocatalyst for the degradation of organic pollutants, which showed much better photocatalytic activity than the sole Ag3PO4 and g-C3N4/Ag3PO4 without NCDs. CDs/{001} BiOI nanosheets photocatalyst was prepared to degrade MO under visible light irradiation, and its degradation efficiency was 4 times higher than the pristine {001} BiOI nanosheets [27]. Generally, CDs is beneficial to enhance photocatalytic performance of the multi-component photocatalyst due to the improved light harvesting capacity and the promoted separation and transfer of photo-generated charge carriers.

In this work, CDs was prepared using semi-coke, a solid waste obtained from oil shale by low-temperature (500–700 °C) carbonization, as carbon source, which is an eco-friendly and energy-saving route to turn waste into valuable. The obtained CDs was used to synthesize a series of CDsAg-AgCl visible-light photocatalysts via two-step electrostatic-assembly approach: 1) CDsAg formation; 2) CDsAg-AgCl formation (without Ag0). Distinct from the physical blending, CDsAg was heterojuncted into AgCl to construct an AgCl-based (CDsAg-AgCl) photocatalyst. The chemical composition, micro-structure, visible-light photocatalytic performance and its stability were comprehensively investigated. The results showed the CDsAg-AgCl photocatalyst achieved significantly enhanced visible-light photocatalytic performances in both dye degradation and hydrogen evolution. Moreover, a possible photocatalytic mechanism was proposed. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first study to utilize CDs to construct a new heterojunction photocatalyst by a novel and convenient strategy, which could be greatly beneficial for the application of visible-light photocatalytic technology in the field of energy and environment.

Sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 95.0–98.9%) and nitric acid (HNO3, 65.0–68.0%) were purchased from Laiyang economic and technological development zone fine chemical plant (Yantai, China) and ammonium hydroxide (NH3·H2O, 25–28%) were purchased from Fuyu Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Sodium chlorate (NaClO3, 99.0%) was supplied by Sigma-Aldrich Reagents Co., Ltd. (US). The semi-coke was supplied generously by Prof. ChunHu Li from the Ocean University of China, which was derived from Jalainur area of Inner Mongolia. Sodium chloride (NaCl), silver nitrate (AgNO3), ammonium oxalate (AO) and isopropanol (IPA) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). MO was purchased from BASF Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, china). All the chemicals were used as received without further purification. Deionized water (DI, resistivity of 18.2 MΩ·cm, 25 °C) obtained through an ultra-pure purification system was used throughout all experiments process.

CDs were prepared from semi-coke via an acidic oxidation route according to the previous reports as depicted in Fig. S1 [28]. In a typical procedure, H2SO4 (35 mL) and HNO3 (20 mL) were mixed in a reaction vessel and cooled to 5 °C by immersion in an ice bath. 1.0 g semi-coke was subsequently added into the cooled mixture under vigorous stirring for two hours. And then 16.5 g NaClO3 was slowly added to the mixture (over a 1 h period) in order to avoid heat surge and the formation of explosive chlorine dioxide gas. After complete dissolution of NaClO3, the mixture was vigorously stirred for 48 h at room temperature. After that, the solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min for 2–3 times. Then the supernatant liquid was adjusted by adding NH3·H2O dropwise until the pH was turned to 6–7. Finally, the obtained solution was further purified by repeated dialysis for more than 2 days until the conductivity of the solution was less than 10 μs/cm, therewith a rotary evaporation process (N–1100D, EYELA, Japan) to acquire the concentrated CDs solution. The CDs solution showed a very strong negative potential of −32.22 mV (Fig. S2). Other characterizations were executed to demonstrate the successful preparation of CDs as showed in Fig. S3.

The CDsAg-AgCl composite photocatalyst was synthesized via two-step electrostatic-assembly approach: 1) CDsAg formation; 2) CDsAg-AgCl formation. At first, 0.1 mol L−1 AgNO3 solution (15 mL) was added to the CDs solution (10 mL) and a brownish-black flocculent precipitate was formed after silencing for 2 h. Then the obtained precipitate was repeatedly washed with DI water to remove the free ions, which was named as CDsAg (Fig. S4). Then, a certain amount of NaCl solution was slowly added to the CDsAg precipitate and sonicated for 30 min. The product was repeatedly washed with DI water and then lyophilized in a refrigerant drier (SClENTZ-30F, Xianzhi, China) to obtain CDsAg-AgCl powders. To prepare the CDsAg-AgCl photocatalyst with a controllable component, the molar percentage of Cl−/Ag+ was controlled to be 50%, 70%, 80%, 90%, 95%, respectively via adjusting the NaCl solution amount. The resulting sample was simplified to CDsAg-AgClx, in which x represented molar percentage of Cl−/Ag+.

For comparison, the sole AgCl photocatalyst was prepared by adding dropwise equivalent AgNO3 solution into NaCl solution under dark condition. Meanwhile, the CDsAg+AgCl photocatalyst was prepared through a simple physical mixing between CDsAg and AgCl.

Zeta potential was measured by zeta potential analyzer (Zetasizer Nano ZS, Malvern, UK). The photoluminescence (PL) measurements of CDs solution were recorded on a fluorescence spectrometer (F-4600, Hitachi, Japan). The crystalline structures of samples were analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD) (D/max 2500 v/pc, Rigaku, Japan) using Cu K α radiation (λ = 1.54050 Å). The infrared absorption spectra were determined by Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR) (Tensor27, Bruker, Germany). The morphologies were observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (S-4800, Hitachi, Japan) and transmission electron microscope (TEM) (H-7650, Hitachi, Japan). Ultraviolet-visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–vis DRS) was recorded using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-4100, Shimadzu, Japan) in the range of 200–800 nm by using BaSO4 as a reflectance standard. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were measured by a photoelectron spectrometer (ESCALAB 250XI, Thermo, US) with a monochromatic X-ray source of Mg K α, calibrated internally by C 1s peak at 284.6 eV for the surface adventitious carbon. Transient photocurrent responses and electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) were recorded by an electrochemical workstation (CHI 760D, ChenHua, China) with a standard three-electrode system under Xe lamp irradiation (300 W, λ > 420 nm). The working electrode was prepared as a typical process [29]: the as-prepared photocatalysts, ethanol and Nafion were mixed to form a homogeneous suspension and coated onto an indium tin oxide (ITO) glass with a light irradiation area of 1.5 cm2 and dried at 45 °C overnight. The electrolyte solution was 0.1 mol L−1 Na2SO4 solution. EIS spectra were performed over a frequency range of 100 kHz to 100 mHz.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of photocatalytic activities and photostability
ce:section.ce:para: MO aqueous solution was used as model pollutant to assess the photocatalytic activities under visible light illumination (λ > 420 nm) [30,31]. The photodegradation experiments were conducted in 100 mL of Pyrex double-jacket reactor of a photochemical reactor. The distance between the light source and the surface of the reaction solution was 10 cm. A water bath connected with a pump was used to maintain the reaction temperature at 16 ± 2 °C. The external visible light irradiation was supplied by a 300 W Xe lamp (CEL-HXF300, CEAULIGHT, China) with a 420 nm cut-off filter provided visible-light irradiation.

The typical procedure of photodegradation experiment was followed the previous reports [32]. The photodegradation performances of the photocatalysts (20 mg) were examined by monitoring the degradation of MO (10 mg L−1, 50 mL) in a beaker under constant stirring at room temperature. Prior to irradiation, the reaction mixture was stirred in the dark for 30 min to establish an adsorption-desorption equilibrium between the photocatalyst and the pollutant (Fig. S5). Then, at selected time intervals, 4 mL samples were collected and centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 3 min to remove the photocatalyst particles. After that, the MO concentration was analyzed using a UV–vis light spectrophotometer (UV-2450, Hitachi, Japan) by recording the variation of the absorbance at 476 nm. For comparison, a blank test was carried out on an MO aqueous solution without any photocatalyst under the same condition. In order to ensure the reproducibility of the results, at least duplicated experiments were carried out for each condition to acquire the average value. To determine the reusability and stability of the photocatalyst, the recycle experiment was implemented, in which the photocatalyst was collected by centrifugation from reaction system, washed with ethanol and DI water and dried before being re-dispersed in the fresh MO solution for the next cycle.

The photocatalytic hydrogen evolution performance was measured in a photoreactor vial with a total volume of 250 mL. A 300 W Xe lamp (Microsolar300, PerfectLight, China) with a 420 nm UV-cutoff filter was used as a visible light source to trigger the photocatalytic reaction. The photocatalytic reaction was performed in 100 mL water suspension of photocatalyst (50 mg) with 20% methanol as sacrificial reagents. The hydrogen product from the aqueous solution was analyzed by gas chromatography (GC-7806, Beijing, China). The carrier gas was high purity nitrogen.

The CDsAg-AgCl photocatalyst was synthesized by two-step electrostatic-assembly, as shown in Fig. 1 . As the prepared CDs solution was a stable colloid with strongly negative charge (Fig. S2), the CDsAg floccule was formed via first-step electrostatic assembly between Ag+ and CDs when AgNO3 was added into the CDs solution. After washing, the electrolyte solution NaCl was added into the CDsAg floccule solution, destroying its stability and separating a portion Ag+ from CDs. The free Ag+ recombined with Cl− to form AgCl via second-step electrostatic assembly. And then the free CDs was wrapped by AgCl. So the unseparated CDsAg and formed AgCl accomplished CDsAg-AgCl heterostructure photocatalysts.

Typical morphologies of as-prepared CDsAg-AgCl photocatalysts and other raw materials were shown in Fig. 2 and Fig. S6. The CDs was well-dispersed spherical with particle size of approximate 5–20 nm (Fig. S6a and S6d) and CDsAg was irregular geometry with a certain aggregation (Fig. S6b and S6e). The AgCl showed bulk structure with size of about 1–3 μm (Fig. S6c and S6f). While the as-prepared CDsAg-AgCl presented the particle size of only 80–200 nm (Fig. 2b) and relatively larger specific surface area (Fig. S7), which may provide more active sites to contact the pollutants molecular sufficiently for the enhanced photocatalytic activity [33]. In CDsAg-AgCl photocatalyst composite, the CDs and CDsAg was well anchored on the AgCl bulk (Fig. 2c). The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (Fig. 2d) also revealed two kinds of lattice spacing of 0.27 nm and 0.32 nm, which corresponded to the cubic AgCl (200) planes and CDs carbon (002) planes, respectively [27,34]. The morphological variation demonstrated the heterostructure was formed in CDsAg-AgCl photocatalyst system, which was important for the separation of photogenerated electrons and holes, as well as the enhancement of visible light absorption.

The typical XRD patterns of as-prepared CDs, CDsAg, AgCl and CDsAg-AgCl90 were showed in Fig. 3a. For CDs, a strong diffraction peak centered at ca. 32.2°, which can be attributed to the highly disordered carbon atoms [35]. The XRD pattern of CDsAg showed no obvious diffraction peak, indicating that there was no Ag0 during the formation of CDsAg. For pure AgCl, the major diffraction peaks marked with “•” at 2θ = 27.8°, 32.3°, 46.3°, 54.9°, 57.6°, 67.4°, 74.5° and 76.6° can be recognized as the reflections (111), (200), (220), (311), (222), (400), (331) and (420) planes, respectively, which was in accordance with the face-cubic AgCl crystals (JCPDS card No.31–1238) [36]. In addition, all diffraction peaks of AgCl were still appeared in the CDsAg-AgCl90 photocatalyst, highlighting the AgCl-based photocatalyst. Fig. 3b showed the FTIR spectra of CDs, AgCl powder, CDsAg and a series of CDsAg-AgCl photocatalysts. The CDs showed strong absorption peaks such as −OH stretching (3000 to 3500 cm−1), CO stretching (1401 cm−1) and C-O-C stretching vibration (1055 cm−1) resulting from oxidation by H2SO4 and HNO3 during preparation [37]. CDsAg and CDsAg-AgCl photocatalysts showed the similar absorption peaks with CDs, confirming the existence of CDs active component in the photocatalyst. Therefore, the CDsAg-AgCl photocatalyst focused on the merits of both CDs and AgCl.

The chemical compositions of the as-prepared photocatalysts were characterized by XPS spectra, as shown in Fig. 4 . From the XPS survey spectra (Fig. 4a), the elements of C and O were detected in CDs, CDsAg and CDsAg-AgCl90 and the elements of Cl and Ag were detected in both AgCl and CDsAg-AgCl90 photocatalyst, which demonstrated that the active components of CDs and AgCl existed in CDsAg-AgCl composite. No other impurity peaks were detected, proving the high purity of the resulting sample. The high-resolution C 1s spectra of all samples can be divided into three peaks located at 284.8 eV, 285.8 eV and 288.8 eV, which can be ascribed to CC or CH bond, CO bond and CO bond in CDs respectively (Fig. 4b). The core levels of Cl 2p displayed double peaks located at 198.2 and 199.8 eV, which can be assigned to the characteristic doublets of Cl 2p3/2 and Cl 2p1/2 in AgCl, respectively (Fig. 4c) [38,39]. In Fig. 4d, two peaks of Ag 3d at 367.3 and 373.3 eV were assigned to the Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2 [40], ascribing to Ag+ in AgCl, which indicated the same status of the silver ion in photocatalyst system.

UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra can provide significant information about the ability of photocatalysts in generating electron-hole (e−/h+) pairs under the illumination [41]. As depicted in Fig. 5a. The absorption edge of CDsAg-AgCl photocatalyst was at about 450 nm, showing a slight red shift compared with the value in reported literature [18] due to the interaction between CDsAg and AgCl. This may support that no Ag nanocrystals existed in the prepared system. And the CDsAg-AgCl exhibited broader and stronger absorption in the whole interested spectrum window compared to pristine AgCl and CDsAg, which illustrated that the heterostructure can enhance the visible light harvesting capacity.

The band gap energy of semiconductors can be estimated by Kubelka−Munk transformation [42], as followed:
(1)
αhν = β
hν − Eg
n 2
where α, h, ν, β, and E g were absorption coefficient, Planck constant, the light frequency, proportionality constant and band gap energy, respectively. n depends on the characteristics of the transition in a semiconductor. In this system, n is 4 for an indirect transition [43]. Thus, the band gap energy (E g) can be estimated from the intercept of the tangents to the plots of (αhν)1/2 vs photon energy as shown in Fig. 5b. The band gap of AgCl was 3.25 eV which was consistent with the value in the literature [41]. The CDsAg-AgCl presented the minimum band gap of 3.08 eV, indicating that the heterostructure can alter the band gap of AgCl to make it absorb visible light more easily.

The photoelectrochemical analysis was used to explain the photocatalytic electron transfer mechanism [44]. From Fig. 5c, AgCl produced anode photocurrent, while other samples contained CDs produced conspicuous cathode photocurrent, which was different with most of the reported photocatalysts. It was inferred that the CDs participated in the formation of heterostructure and may change the type of semiconductor for photocatalyst. The CDsAg-AgCl90 photocatalyst presented the highest photocurrent intensity among all samples, which revealed heterojunction structure facilitated the transfer-separation of photo-generated electron-hole pairs. After eight light on-off cycles, the photocurrent response of CDsAg-AgCl90 photocatalyst presented slight attenuation, indicating its excellent stability of heterostructure photocatalyst. As EIS spectra depicted in Fig. 5d, the CDsAg-AgCl90 photocatalyst exhibited a semicircular arc with the smallest radius, suggesting lower charge transfer resistance [45,46]. The measurements confirmed that the heterostructure between CDsAg and AgCl was beneficial for higher transfer efficiency and lower recombination efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs.

The photocatalytic degradation performance of the prepared AgCl-based heterostructure photocatalyst were evaluated and the results were displayed in Fig. 6a and Fig. S8. Prior to irradiation, the reaction mixture was magnetically stirred in the dark for 0.5 h to reach an adsorption–desorption equilibrium (Fig. S5). The blank experiment without photocatalysts was also executed and MO was barely degraded under visible-light irradiation, indicating negligible direct photolysis of MO. Among all of the samples, CDsAg-AgCl photocatalysts showed the best degradation efficiency of more than 90%, which was approximately 2 times higher than that of the physical mixture CDsAg+AgCl, demonstrating the importance of the heterojunction interface among CDsAg and AgCl. In addition, the degradation efficiency of the CDsAg-AgCl photocatalysts can be adjusted by the different molar percentage of Cl−/Ag+. Especially, for the CDsAg-AgCl90, 90.6% of the MO was degraded at the first 30 min and totally degraded within 75 min, exhibiting the remarkable photocatalytic degradation performance. However, the degradation efficiency would be declined the corresponding degradation efficiency was declined. It can be ascribed that AgCl compound might be formed with the combination of excessive Cl− with more Ag+ to cover the reactive sites and thus damage the heterojunction interfaces, which would lead to the attenuation of photocatalytic performance [47]. The experimental data was fitted by the pseudo-first-order kinetic as expressed by Eq. (2) (Fig. S8b) [48]. The photodegrading rate constant (k, min−1) of CDsAg-AgCl heterostructure photocatalyst was significantly superior to the other samples. Especially, the CDsAg-AgCl90 exhibited the highest photodegradation rate constant of as high as 0.069 min−1, which was about 42.3, 8.9, 10.6 faster than those of the pure CDs, AgCl, CDsAg+AgCl, respectively. So the formation of CDsAg-AgCl heterostructure photocatalysts with an optimal molar percentage of Cl−/Ag+ can facilitate photocatalytic degradation of dyes.
(2)
− ln
C
C 0
= kt

The concentration and composition of pollutants are vital to for practical wastewater treatment. So, the effect of MO initial concentration on the photocatalytic degradation performance over CDsAg-AgCl90 photocatalyst was investigated and the results were illustrated in Fig. 6b and Fig. S9a. With the MO concentration increase from 10 mg L−1 to 30 mg L−1, the degradation efficiency decreased from 100% to 60.7% within 120 min. It may be inferred that more MO molecules due to increased MO concentration can compete with the degrading intermediates of MO molecules, leading to incomplete degradation of more MO molecules [49]. Meanwhile, the effect of inorganic salt (NaCl) in MO solution on the photodegradation efficiency was also investigated. From Fig. 6c and Fig. S9b, the presence of NaCl inhibited the MO degradation process and decreased the degradation rate. This might be attributed that the increased ion strength augmented the electrostatic repulsion between ions, which rendered the reduced surface adsorption site of the photocatalyst. In spite of this, the degradation efficiency can still reach more than 80% within 120 min in case of 10 g L−1 NaCl, indicating that the prepared photocatalyst possessed great potential for being used in practical water treatment. In addition, the stability of a photocatalyst is also very important in practical applications. The reusability and stability of CDsAg-AgCl90 photocatalyst were investigated by recycle photocatalytic experiments with MO initial concentration of 10 mg L−1 and NaCl concentration of 0 g L−1 in Fig. 6d. The amount of CDsAg-AgCl90 was kept the same to maintain the same initial degradation rate. The degradation efficiency of MO reached 100% within 90 min for the first four recycles and still reached more than 94% for the fifth cycle, demonstrating that CDsAg-AgCl photocatalyst had a high photocatalytic degradation stability. The physical adsorptions of MO molecular over the surface of photocatalysts could keep reactive molecular away from the active sites that may result in the slight decrease in photocatalytic degradation efficiency during the cycling run [50].

It is known that the •O2 −, h+ and •OH are supposed as three major reactive species in photocatalytic reaction process [51–53]. The change of photocatalytic efficiency can reflect the effects of the corresponding reactive species on the photocatalytic process by adding different scavengers into reaction systems to trap corresponding reactive species [54]. In order to investigate the dominant reactive species in prepared photocatalyst system, trapping experiments were carried out with AO, IPA and N2 purging using as h+, •OH and •O2 − scavengers, respectively [55]. As depicted in Fig. 6e and f, the photodegradation efficiency was barely affected with the introduction of IPA into the system, but the degradation efficiency was whittled down into 26% and 69.5% within 30 min after the addition of AO and purging of N2 into the reaction system, respectively. This meant that h+ and •O2 − were the major reactive species for the CDsAg-AgCl photocatalyst and the influence degree was as the sequence of h+ > > •O2 − > •OH.

To further understand the improved photocatalytic degradation performance of the CDsAg-AgCl heterojunction photocatalysts, a reasonable photocatalytic mechanism was proposed on the basis of the above results, as schematically presented in Fig. 7 . In order to investigate transfer-separation of electron-hole pairs of CDsAg-AgCl photocatalyst, it was essential to know the band structure of the components. The conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB) energy levels of a semiconductor material could be calculated by the equations as follows [56,57]:
(3)
ECB = χ −
E e
− 0.5 Eg
(4)
EVB = ECB + Eg
where E
CB , E
VB , χ, E
e and E
g represent the CB and VB energy level, absolute electronegativity, energy of free electrons on the hydrogen scale (4.5 eV vs NHE) and band gap of semiconductor, respectively. χ was calculated by the following equation:
(5)
χ =
χ
A

χ
B

where a and b are the number of atoms in the compounds [58]. The CB and VB potentials of AgCl were calculated to be −0.06 and 3.19 eV versus normal hydrogen electrode (NHE), consistent with the literature [41]. The CB and VB potentials of CDsAg were calculated to be 0.25 eV and 3.70 eV, respectively. For individual CDsAg, it was difficult to be excited by absorbing visible light because of broader band gap (3.45 eV according to Fig. 5b), thereby exhibiting a weaker photocatalytic activity (Fig. 6a). However, for CDsAg-AgCl photocatalyst, CDs was wrapped by AgCl to form the heterostructure by interface couple between AgCl and CDsAg. At the beginning of the degradation, the adsorption-desorption equilibrium of CDsAg-AgCl photocatalyst was broken under the irradiation. The AgCl component with lower band gap was activated to generate a large number of e−/h+ pairs. Furthermore, the CB of AgCl was more negative than that of CDsAg, so the photogenerated e− in the AgCl component rapidly transfered to the CB of CDsAg. On the other hand, the left h+ moved from the VB of CDsAg to AgCl due to the well-matched energy levels. Besides, the existence of free CDs could also accelerate the separation and transfer of photogenerated electron-hole, assisting to increase the reactive rate. Meanwhile, the photogenerated holes had a great affinity to capture electrons from adsorbed dye molecules to oxidize organic pollutants directly. In addition, the electrons on the CDsAg not only can react with O2 to produce •O2 −, but also act as the photocatalytic active sites to degrade pollutants. Generally, the improved photocatalytic performance of the CDsAg-AgCl heterostructure photocatalysts was attributed to more harvesting of visible light, the more production of electron-hole pairs and remarkable separation and transfer of photogenerated electron-hole.

The photocatalytic degradation performances of CDsAg-AgCl photocatalyst is compared with the results reported in the literatures on heterojunction photocatalysts for the MO decomposition under visible light irradiation as listed in Table 1 . The prepared CDsAg-AgCl photocatalyst exhibited the better or comparative visible-light photocatalytic degradation performance. The prepared AgCl-based heterostructure photocatalysts is considered to be a promising and competitive photocatalyst for practical application.

To further confirm the excellent photocatalytic performance of CDsAg-AgCl photocatalyst, photocatalytic hydrogen evolution test for water splitting was performed as well. As shown in Fig. 8a, the H2 evolution rate of as high as 617.4 μmol g−1 h−1 can be obtained under visible light. Meanwhile, the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution performance can maintain at least five recycles with no obvious decrease, indicating its good recyclability. The photocatalytic H2 production rate of CDsAg-AgCl photocatalyst in this work is 10.4 times higher than of Ag/AgX/CN composites (up to 59.22 μmol g−1 h−1) as Bai et al. reported [65]. The possible photocatalytic hydrogen evolution mechanism on the sample CDsAg-AgCl was proposed. Under light irradiation, the CDsAg-AgCl could be excited to produce e− and h+. The e− in the VB could be excited to the CB, leaving the same amount of h+ in the VB [66]. Moreover, the photoexcited electrons in the CB of AgCl can transferred to the CB of CDsAg to achieve a balance. Meanwhile, the photogenerated holes in the VB of CDsAg can also transfer to the VB of AgCl. Therefore, photogenerated e−/h+ pairs can be efficiently separated by the formed heterojunction between AgCl and CDsAg. Finally, the e− could react with H2O adsorbed onto photocatalyst and produce H2.

As the AgX based composites for water splitting H2 evolution are rarely reported, we also compared as-prepared CDsAg-AgCl photocatalyst with other Ag-based materials in the literatures (Fig. 8b), which exhibited a quite competitive result. This may also show potential for CDsAg-AgCl heterojunction photocatalyst to be used in H2 production application.

In summary, via in-situ electrostatic-assembly method of the CDs, a series of novel efficient AgCl-based heterojunction photocatalysts was synthesized successfully for the first time. The characterization revealed the formation of CDsAg-AgCl heterostructure with the interface couple between CDsAg and AgCl. The CDsAg-AgCl heterostructure photocatalyst exhibited superior photocatalytic performance and stability in both dye degradation and hydrogen evolution under visible light irradiation. The photocatalytic degradation rate constant of MO by the CDsAg-AgCl90 was 0.069 min−1, which was about 42.3, 8.9 and 10.6 times faster than those of the pure CDs, AgCl and physically mixed CDsAg+AgCl, respectively. It also yielded an excellent hydrogen evolution efficiency of 617.4 μmolg−1 h−1, which showed comparative competitiveness compared to other Ag-based composites. Meanwhile, a possible photocatalytic mechanism was proposed, where CDsAg-AgCl heterojunction structure was conductive to produce more photo-generated electron-hole pairs and facilitate their transfer-separation of pairs to promote the photocatalytic performance. The photocatalyst is promising and competitive for wastewater treatment and energy regeneration. This work could provide a new route to prepare novel high-efficient photocatalysts for the energy and environmental application.
